rationale: RootingHelpers.cs is pre-existing code outside the diff; an empty dependency list when CheckConstraints fails is the intended dataflow design (invalid shapes deliberately not rooted) — out-of-scope.

Verified against the constraint loop: composite canon value-type shapes (MyStruct<string> vs MyStruct<__Canon>) fall past CanCastToConstraintWithCanon (which only handles canon definition types) into the pre-existing value-type prefilter, which returns false before CanCastTo/IsCanonEquivalent could match — conservative under-match in the #126604 class.

The Length==0 early-return vs doc asymmetry is real, but every call site ORs IsCanonEquivalent with IsEquivalentTo (or checks == first), so identical non-generic pairs are already handled — doc-precision only.
Equal arity is guaranteed by HasSameTypeDefinition and == on interned TypeDescs is the codebase idiom; missing Debug.Assert is a defensive-style note.
True — IsSpecialTypeMeetingConstraint has no doc comment in the diff — but a missing doc comment is trivial.

Verified: TypeSystemConstraintsHelpers.NonCanon.cs keeps 'using System.Diagnostics;' that its false-returning stubs never use — trivial cleanup miss.

Verified doc mismatches: the pointers/byrefs/fnptr sentence in IsCanonicalCastTarget has no corresponding code check (Universal branch returns true unconditionally) and IsCanonicalTypeArgMatch's summary omits its structural/recursive half — doc-level.

Exactly the jkotas thread: System.Private.TypeLoader.csproj gets the REAL CastingHelper.Canon.cs (diff lines 9-11), silently changing runtime type-loader CanCastTo semantics with no test and only the 'include all .Canon.cs files' rule as justification.

Verifiable against the test diff: no covariant (out T) fixture appears in the new tests, and the base-chain canon match (ComplexGenericConstraint2) is only hit at zero-hop — real shallow-coverage gaps on the casting integration points.

The diff's own doc comment declares pointers/byrefs/function pointers invalid as instantiation arguments, so the direct delegate*<__Canon,...> arg scenario cannot arise; any residual nested case fails conservatively (returns false = pre-PR behavior), not unsoundly.

Matches must_flag (c): the broadened CanCastTo has zero coverage outside constraint checking and none for compareTypesForCast/devirtualization consumers with canonical inputs; traced the tests — CanCastToConstraintWithCanon intercepts canon-target cases before IsCanonicalCastTarget, so its true-arms plausibly never execute.
Fixture placement/naming quibbles in the test assembly — pure style.

Cluster self-refutes live impact: the new checks are additive short-circuits, so IsGCPointer being false for a constrained GenericParameterDesc just falls through to pre-existing logic (pre-PR behavior) — consistency note.

Verified: the Universal->true / Specific->IsGCPointer kernel is re-encoded in IsCanonicalCastTarget, CanCastToConstraintWithCanon, and IsCanonicalTypeArgMatch — real DRY/drift concern, but structural/maintainability, not a defect.

The precise primary mechanism: optimistic canon-widened CanCastTo answers flow through CorInfoImpl.compareTypesForCast as TypeCompareState.Must (its own table says May), so JIT/crossgen2 folds required runtime casts — the exact type-safety miscompile that produced #127259 and the revert.

Spot-verified: __Canon is a GC pointer so the class-constraint Assert.True passes via the pre-existing IsGCPointer short-circuit, and the contravariant IGen cases pass via ordinary variance (__Canon.CanCastTo(object) is true pre-PR) — assertions vacuously credit the new code.
Explicitly concedes the semicolon body declarations are valid at this LangVersion (correctly avoids the safe-bodyless-types false positive); remaining claim is file-local style only.

Verified in the test diff: the block calls IGen<in T> contravariant then says 'invariant arg position', and the 'Parameterized canonical types (__Canon[])' header's body never constructs __Canon[] (uses int[]+canon, duplicating an earlier case) — wrong comments, but comment-level.

The UC<->Canon cross-form disjuncts are consistent with the PR's intended optimistic 'castable for SOME instantiation' semantics (UC may stand for a ref type); 'unexplained' is a documentation note, not a distinct defect.

Refuted: System.Private.TypeLoader does not compile TypeSystemConstraintsHelpers.cs at all (a missing partial flavor would be a compile error and the project builds), so no Canon/NonCanon pairing is required — the exact 'missing matching pair' FP named in the brief.

Verified in diff: only MakeGenericTypeSite receives NormalizeInstantiation(); the parallel MakeGenericMethod dataflow path is left checking constraints on denormalized shapes — plausible incomplete-fix asymmetry, untested, though downstream canonicalization may mitigate.

If __UniversalCanon.IsValueType (which is how the UC struct-constraint test passes through the caller guard), the (!IsValueType || IsNullable) gate is false for UC and the NotNullableValueTypeConstraint arm can never fire — dead arm in new code, minor impact.

Doc-phrasing issues only: the summary reads as a caller precondition though the method is invoked unconditionally (and correctly returns false otherwise); the runtime-validation claim is a comment assertion.
Naming-drift observations (type vs thisType, IsCanonEquivalent short form, Check* convention) — pure style.

Verified: every new test calls CheckConstraints() with no InstantiationContext while the production caller in HandleCallAction.cs passes new InstantiationContext(typeInst, methodInst) — real, narrow coverage gap.

The orderings are genuinely load-bearing (CanCastToConstraintWithCanon before the value-type check saves UC from the IsValueType prefilter; IsCanonicalCastTarget before the category switch), but the ask is comments — doc-level.

Real design gap: flavor selection is undocumented and silent-on-wrong-choice (compile errors only catch missing/double includes) — the mechanism that actually bit — but stated abstractly, naming neither definite-decision consumers (not primary) nor a specific over-broad project (not h1).
